brief_block: The Second Circuit established controlling precedent in U.S. Bank Trust National Ass'n v. American Airlines, Inc. (In re AMR Corp.), holding that automatic acceleration triggered by bankruptcy filings changes the maturity date from the original stated date to the petition date, precluding make-whole premium claims.  In re AMR Corp., 730 F.3d 88 (2013)
opinion_block: American's bankruptcy petition triggered a default, and this default automatically accelerated the debt. That acceleration 'change[d] the date of maturity from some point in the future . . . to an earlier date based on the debtor's default under the contract.' When the event of default occurred and the debt accelerated, the new maturity date for the debt was November 29, 2011. Consequently, American's attempt to repay the debt in October 2012 was not a voluntary prepayment because '[p]repayment can only occur prior to the maturity date.'
finding_analysis: The brief's characterization of AMR's holding is accurate. The Second Circuit held that the indenture's automatic acceleration provision, triggered by American's voluntary bankruptcy filing, moved the maturity date forward to the petition date (November 29, 2011), so that the later payment was post-maturity rather than a prepayment. Because the indenture conditioned the Make-Whole Amount on voluntary prepayment before maturity, no premium was owed. The brief captures both the mechanism (automatic acceleration shifts maturity) and the consequence (no make-whole) that the court adopted.

brief_block: The court adopted the reasoning from In re Solutia that "[p]repayment can only occur prior to the maturity date," and concluded that when debt accelerates upon bankruptcy filing, "the new maturity date for the debt was November 29, 2011."  In re AMR Corp., 730 F.3d 88 (2013)
opinion_block: When the event of default occurred and the debt accelerated, the new maturity date for the debt was November 29, 2011. Consequently, American's attempt to repay the debt in October 2012 was not a voluntary prepayment because '[p]repayment can only occur prior to the maturity date.' In re Solutia, 379 B.R. at 488. . . . The case law dealing with similar automatic-acceleration-upon-bankruptcy clauses in indenture agreements supports this reading. In re Solutia Inc. involved notes held by the Bank of New York and governed by an indenture agreement. . . . Because the 2009 Notes were automatically accelerated, any payment at this time would not be a prepayment.
finding_analysis: Both quoted strings appear verbatim in the opinion, and the brief's framing matches the court's analytical move. The Second Circuit quoted Solutia's 'Prepayment can only occur prior to the maturity date' proposition directly, then applied that reasoning to hold that the new maturity date for American's accelerated debt was November 29, 2011 (the petition date). The opinion explicitly treats Solutia as supporting authority whose reasoning the court adopts to reach its conclusion, so the brief's characterization is accurate.

brief_block: The Second Circuit reinforced this principle in Matter of MPM Silicones, L.L.C., rejecting noteholders' arguments that issuing replacement notes under a Chapter 11 plan constituted an "optional redemption" triggering make-whole premiums.  Matter of MPM Silicones, L.L.C., 874 F.3d 787 (2017)
opinion_block: The Senior-Lien Notes holders' principal argument is that they are entitled to the make-whole premium because when MPM issued the replacement notes under the Plan, it 'redeemed' the Notes 'at its option' prior to maturity. This argument fails for the same reasons we rejected nearly identical arguments in In re AMR Corp., 730 F.3d 88 (2d Cir. 2013). . . . Moreover, even assuming MPM's issuance of the replacement notes was a 'redemption,' it would not have been 'at [MPM's] option,' as required to trigger the Optional Redemption Clauses.
finding_analysis: The brief accurately characterizes the Second Circuit's holding. The court expressly rejected the Senior-Lien Notes holders' argument that issuing replacement notes under the Chapter 11 plan was an optional redemption triggering the make-whole premium, both because post-acceleration payments are post-maturity (per AMR) and because the replacement notes were issued automatically by operation of the Acceleration Clauses rather than at MPM's option. Note on caption: CourtListener flagged a case-name mismatch because the appeal is docketed as Momentive Performance Materials Inc. v. BOKF, NA while the brief cites it as 'Matter of MPM Silicones, L.L.C.'; the opinion confirms these are the same case (MPM is the debtor-subsidiary whose Chapter 11 plan and 2012 Indentures are at issue throughout), so the mismatch is a false positive.

brief_block: The court held that payments following automatic acceleration are not made "at [MPM's] option" because "the obligation to issue the replacement notes came about automatically by operation of separate indenture provisions, the Automatic Acceleration Clauses."  Matter of MPM Silicones, L.L.C., 874 F.3d 787 (2017)
opinion_block: Moreover, even assuming MPM's issuance of the replacement notes was a 'redemption,' it would not have been 'at [MPM's] option,' as required to trigger the Optional Redemption Clauses. Rather, the obligation to issue the replacement notes came about automatically by operation of separate indenture provisions, the Automatic Acceleration Clauses. A payment made mandatory by operation of an automatic acceleration clause is not one made at MPM's option.
finding_analysis: Both quoted phrases appear verbatim in the opinion and the brief's framing tracks the court's reasoning precisely. The Second Circuit held that issuance of replacement notes under the Plan could not be 'at [MPM's] option' because it was compelled by the Automatic Acceleration Clauses rather than chosen by the debtor, so the Optional Redemption Clauses (the trigger for the make-whole) were not activated. The quote-check flagged one quote as 0.82 similarity, but that reflects the bracketed editorial insertion '[MPM's]' rather than any substantive deviation; the phrases match the opinion's text. Caption note: 'Matter of MPM Silicones, L.L.C.' and 'Momentive Performance Materials Inc. v. BOKF, NA' refer to the same Second Circuit appeal, so the CourtListener name-mismatch flag is a false positive.

brief_block: In AMR, the Second Circuit rejected arguments that refinancing to access better rates constituted voluntary redemption when the indenture's acceleration provisions had already operated automatically.  In re AMR Corp., 730 F.3d 88 (2013)
opinion_block: U.S. Bank argues, finally, that regardless whether American's November 2011 bankruptcy petition was an Event of Default triggering debt acceleration, American's effort in October 2012 to pay off its debt constitutes a Section 2.11 voluntary redemption for which Section 3.02 provides for payment of a Make-Whole Amount. The bankruptcy court rejected this argument, holding that the Debtors' proposed payment is a 'post-maturity date repayment, not a prepayment' . . . . We agree. . . . U.S. Bank argues, next, that Section 3.03, by its terms, only excludes payment of a Make-Whole Amount 'as a consequence of or in connection with an Event of Default or the acceleration of the Equipment Notes,' and that the post-maturity repayment that American now attempts is not, in fact, 'a consequence of or in connection with' either its bankruptcy filing or debt acceleration but is, instead, an attempt to take advantage of low interest rates. We need not parse the meanings of 'consequence' or 'connection' to reject U.S. Bank's interpretation.
finding_analysis: The brief's description matches the opinion. American moved in October 2012 to refinance its debt 'citing low interest rates available in the market,' and U.S. Bank argued that such opportunistic refinancing should be treated as a voluntary redemption owing a Make-Whole Amount. The Second Circuit rejected that argument, holding the payment was a post-maturity repayment governed by Section 3.03 rather than a Section 2.11 voluntary redemption, because automatic acceleration under Section 4.02(a)(i) had already advanced the maturity date to the petition date. The brief's phrase 'the indenture's acceleration provisions had already operated automatically' tracks the court's reasoning about Section 4.02(a)(i)'s self-executing effect on the Section 4.01(g) bankruptcy default.
